content.assessment: This proposal contracts the selective track's capacity and rigor thresholds but preserves the underlying tracking age and institutional bifurcation. The lens evidence indicates that the equity effects of early tracking are structural (tied to the existence and age of the selection point, F-live-finnish_reform-05) rather than purely a function of track size. Shrinking the selective track without changing the age at which it operates or the comprehensiveness of provision may concentrate advantage among a smaller, still-earlier-selected population, resembling the mechanism identified in Finland whereby narrowing formal channels does not eliminate stratification pressure but can redirect it (F-live-finnish_reform-08, F-live-finnish_reform-09). The proposal's own stated risk -- that non-state schools could absorb displaced demand -- parallels the Finnish pattern in which choice-driven segregation persisted through alternative channels (residential/school choice) even as the formal system remained nominally comprehensive.
opportunities: Demographic decline as political cover mirrors the kind of institutional condition under which structural reforms (e.g., Finland's 1960s-70s reform) became politically durable, suggesting a possible sequencing opportunity to combine contraction with structural reform while demographic pressure provides cover
strengths: Reduces the absolute footprint of the early-selection mechanism, which is a necessary (if not sufficient) precondition for reducing the population exposed to the inequality-associated mechanism identified in cross-national tracking-age comparisons (F-live-finnish_reform-05)
threats: Displacement to church/foundation schools outside state jurisdiction parallels the Finnish finding that middle-class families route around comprehensive constraints via choice mechanisms, producing segregation effects that a formal capacity policy cannot fully control (F-live-finnish_reform-09)
weaknesses: Retains the age-10/12 selection point itself; the lens evidence associates the timing of first selection, not merely its capacity, with the differential outcomes (F-live-finnish_reform-05, F-live-finnish_reform-06)

Contraction without accompanying comprehensive redesign of the non-selective track for those excluded risks concentrating remaining scarce places among the most-advantaged applicants, echoing how Finnish 'emphasized classes' concentrate favorable placement without a corresponding change in the achievement gap they generate (F-live-finnish_reform-08)
